title: Test statistic and P-value in chi-square tests with two-way tables
description: Calculate the test statistic and p-value in a chi square test for independence or homogeneity.
topic_path: Chi square tests for relationships (homogeneity or independence)
Test statistic and P-value in chi-square tests with two-way tables

assessment_items.question_content:
A large company was curious about job satisfaction between employees at two different locations. They surveyed a random sample of $50$ employees from each location about their overall job satisfaction. Here are the responses and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Satisfaction vs. location ||
| Location A | Location B | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Satisfied | $28$ | $31$ | $59$
 | $29.5$ | $29.5$ | 
No opinion | $10$ | $4$ | $14$
 | $7.0$ | $7.0$ | 
Dissatisfied | $12$ | $15$| $27$
 | $13.5$ | $13.5$ |
**Total** | $50$ | $50$ | $100$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of homogeneity. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| **Chi-square test: Satisfaction vs. location** ||
| Location A | Location B | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Satisfied | $28$ | $31$ | $59$
 | $29.5$ | $29.5$ | 
No opinion | $10$ | $4$ | $14$
 | $7.0$ | $7.0$ | 
Dissatisfied | $12$ | $15$| $27$
 | $13.5$ | $13.5$ |
**Total** | $50$ | $50$ | $100$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(28-29.5)^2}{29.5}+...+\dfrac{(15-13.5)^2}{13.5} \\\\
&\approx 0.076+...+0.167 \\\\
&\approx 3.06
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=3.06$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{3}$ row variables and $\maroonD{2}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD3-1)(\maroonD2-1)=2$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases vertically then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 2. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 3.06. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/69d45bda76c04766918c1a5e4841c51d547f35e4)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=2$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=3.06$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.25 and 0.20 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 2 is highlighted. 0.25 and 2 intersect at value 2.773. 0.20 and 2 intersect at value 3.219.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b597f6073f5436e6579360060a4d2da34d37598c)

Since our test statistic is between $2.773$ and $3.219$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.20$ and $0.25$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>3.06$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 3.06 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 2 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.217
\end{align}$

###Answer

The test statistic is $\chi^2=3.06$, and the P-value is between $0.20$ and $0.25$.

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=3.06; \\
&0.20 <\text{P-value} < 0.25
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A researcher wanted to see if different breeds of dogs preferred toys of certain colors. The researcher presented each dog with $3$ toys, identical except that each was a different color, and recorded which toy the dog played with the most in a set time period. Here are the outcomes and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Breed vs. toy color ||
 | Yellow | Purple | Black | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Beagle | $16$ | $30$ | $14$ | $60$
 | $24$ | $23$ | $13$ | 
Collie | $27$ | $23$ | $10$ | $60$
 | $24$ | $23$ | $13$ | 
Husky | $29$ | $16$ | $15$ | $60$
 | $24$ | $23$ | $13$ | 
**Total** | $72$ | $69$ | $39$ | $180$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test for homogeneity. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Breed vs. toy color ||
 | Yellow | Purple | Black | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Beagle | $16$ | $30$ | $14$ | $60$
 | $24$ | $23$ | $13$ | 
Collie | $27$ | $23$ | $10$ | $60$
 | $24$ | $23$ | $13$ | 
Husky | $29$ | $16$ | $15$ | $60$
 | $24$ | $23$ | $13$ | 
**Total** | $72$ | $69$ | $39$ | $180$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(16-24)^2}{24}+...+\dfrac{(15-13)^2}{13} \\\\
&\approx 2.667+...+0.308 \\\\
&\approx 9.421
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=9.421$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{3}$ row variables and $\maroonD{3}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{3}-1)(\maroonD{3}-1)=4$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases sharply then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 4. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 9.42. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8492d284be7ff20c8102663d05e8d80968005f68)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=4$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=9.421$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.10 and 0.05 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 4 is highlighted. 0.10 and 4 intersect at value 7.779. 0.05 and 4 intersect at value 9.488.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a03245c3fd49ed0de67bedbefbb1bc49bd8be180)

Since our test statistic is between $7.779$ and $9.488$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.05$ and $0.10$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>9.421$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 9.421 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 4 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.051
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=9.421; \\
&0.05 <\text{P-value} < 0.10
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A market researcher for a communications company took a sample of $300$ customers to see if those with children were more or less likely to pay for upgraded internet speeds.  Here are the outcomes and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Number of children vs. upgrade ||
 | No children | $1$ child | $2$ or more children | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Upgrade | $38$ | $7$ | $3$ | $48$
 | $32$ | $9.6$ | $6.4$ | 
No upgrade | $162$ | $53$ | $37$ | $252$
 | $168$ | $50.4$ | $33.6$ | 
**Total** | $200$ | $60$ | $40$ | $300$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of association. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Number of children vs. upgrade ||
 | No children | $1$ child | $2$ or more children | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Upgrade | $38$ | $7$ | $3$ | $48$
 | $32$ | $9.6$ | $6.4$ | 
No upgrade | $162$ | $53$ | $37$ | $252$
 | $168$ | $50.4$ | $33.6$ | 
**Total** | $200$ | $60$ | $40$ | $300$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(38-32)^2}{32}+...+\dfrac{(37-33.6)^2}{33.6} \\\\
&\approx 1.125+...+0.344 \\\\
&\approx 4.327
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=4.327$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{2}$ row variables and $\maroonD{3}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{2}-1)(\maroonD{3}-1)=2$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases vertically then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 2. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 4.33. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/44d11c348be36ddf1c0715565116f78e2ba0c283)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=2$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=4.327$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.15 and 0.10 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 2 is highlighted. 0.15 and 2 intersect at value 3.794. 0.10 and 2 intersect at value 4.605.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1d6450714cc2948bcafe18ff140fbfd311554df6)

Since our test statistic is between $3.794$ and $4.605$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.10$ and $0.15$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>4.327$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 4.327 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 2 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.115
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=4.327; \\
&0.10 <\text{P-value} < 0.15
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Plamen is a social media manager for a large company. He takes a random sample of their posts to see if there is a relationship between the time of each post and the number of times the post gets shared.  Here are the outcomes and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Time of post vs. number of shares ||
 | $0$-$50$ | $51$-$100$ | $100+$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Morning | $203$ | $77$ | $50$ | $330$
 | $219$ | $72$ | $39$ | 
Afternoon | $117$ | $36$ | $12$ | $165$
 | $109.5$ | $36$ | $19.5$ | 
Evening | $45$ | $7$ | $3$ | $55$
 | $36.5$ | $12$ | $6.5$ | 
**Total** | $365$ | $120$ | $65$ | $550$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of independence. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Time of post vs. number of shares ||
 | $0$-$50$ | $51$-$100$ | $100+$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Morning | $203$ | $77$ | $50$ | $330$
 | $219$ | $72$ | $39$ | 
Afternoon | $117$ | $36$ | $12$ | $165$
 | $109.5$ | $36$ | $19.5$ | 
Evening | $45$ | $7$ | $3$ | $55$
 | $36.5$ | $12$ | $6.5$ | 
**Total** | $365$ | $120$ | $65$ | $550$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(203-219)^2}{219}+...+\dfrac{(3-6.5)^2}{6.5} \\\\
&\approx 1.169+...+1.885 \\\\
&\approx 13.965
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=13.965$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{3}$ row variables and $\maroonD{3}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{3}-1)(\maroonD{3}-1)=4$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases sharply then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 4. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 13.96. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc25033a367fc6bdc9a1b4a4af72858d82d92600)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=4$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=13.965$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.01 and 0.005 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 4 is highlighted. 0.01 and 4 intersect at value 13.280. 0.005 and 4 intersect at value 14.860.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fc4a3608b30539aa13f9268bb75cc4f11cc5a26c)

Since our test statistic is between $13.28$ and $14.86$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.005$ and $0.01$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>13.965$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 13.965 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 4 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.007
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=13.965; \\
&0.005 <\text{P-value} < 0.01
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A company is testing their new smartwatch. They wonder if alerts that prompt people to exercise cause people to exercise more than they would without the alerts. They recruit $200$ subjects and randomly assign each of them to either receive the alerts or not. Here are amounts of daily activity and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Group vs. minutes of activity ||
 | Alerts | No alerts | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
$0$-$29$ | $48$ | $64$ | $112$
 | $56$ | $56$ | 
$30$-$59$ | $33$ | $27$ | $60$
 | $30$ | $30$ | 
$60+$ | $19$ | $9$ | $28$
 | $14$ | $14$ | 
**Total** | $100$ | $100$ | $200$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test for homogeneity. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Group vs. minutes of activity ||
 | Alerts | No alerts | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
$0$-$29$ | $48$ | $64$ | $112$
 | $56$ | $56$ | 
$30$-$59$ | $33$ | $27$ | $60$
 | $30$ | $30$ | 
$60+$ | $19$ | $9$ | $28$
 | $14$ | $14$ | 
**Total** | $100$ | $100$ | $200$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(48-56)^2}{56}+...+\dfrac{(9-14)^2}{14} \\\\
&\approx 1.143+...+1.786 \\\\
&\approx 6.458
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=6.458$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{3}$ row variables and $\maroonD{2}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{3}-1)(\maroonD{2}-1)=2$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases vertically then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 2. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 6.46.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b82dd66e611901384031f33b695efb6d6482008f)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=2$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=6.458$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.05 and 0.025 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 2 is highlighted. 0.05 and 2 intersect at value 5.991. 0.025 and 2 intersect at value 7.378.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/97e2b1310ddab902316e12c1c984e1c2e149def3)

Since our test statistic is between $5.991$ and $7.378$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.025$ and $0.05$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>6.458$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 6.458 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 2 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.04
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=6.458; \\
&0.025 <\text{P-value} < 0.05
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Market researchers wonder if people in different countries have different preferred sports to watch during the winter Olympics. They take a random sample of people from each country and survey them about their favorite sport to watch.  Here are the responses and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Country vs. favorite winter Olympics sport ||
 | China | India | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Bobsleigh | $8$ | $10$ | $18$
 | $9$ | $9$ | 
Curling | $28$ | $61$ | $89$
 | $44.5$ | $44.5$ | 
Hockey | $39$ | $29$ | $68$
 | $34$ | $34$ | 
Speed skating | $75$ | $50$ | $125$
 | $62.5$ | $62.5$ | 
**Total** | $150$ | $150$ | $300$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test for homogeneity. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Country vs. favorite winter Olympics sport ||
 | China | India | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Bobsleigh | $8$ | $10$ | $18$
 | $9$ | $9$ | 
Curling | $28$ | $61$ | $89$
 | $44.5$ | $44.5$ | 
Hockey | $39$ | $29$ | $68$
 | $34$ | $34$ | 
Speed skating | $75$ | $50$ | $125$
 | $62.5$ | $62.5$ | 
**Total** | $150$ | $150$ | $300$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(8-9)^2}{9}+...+\dfrac{(50-62.5)^2}{62.5} \\\\
&\approx 0.111+...+2.5 \\\\
&\approx 18.928
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=18.928$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{4}$ row variables and $\maroonD{2}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{4}-1)(\maroonD{2}-1)=3$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases sharply then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 3. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 18.93. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4b1181200a72d27390eb981ab79acf9b88c88ba8)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=3$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=18.928$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The column for tail p = 0.0005 and the row for d f = 3 are highlighted. 0.0005 and 3 intersect at value 17.730.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/48f8b947612efe9588977c4e5de0f6a960773ca6)

Since our test statistic is greater than $17.73$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is less than $0.0005$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>18.928$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 18.928 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 3 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.0003
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=18.928; \\
&\text{P-value} < 0.0005
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of researchers wondered whether vegetarians favored different seasons than non-vegetarians. The researchers took a random sample of people and surveyed them about their favorite season and whether or not they were vegetarian.  Here are the responses and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Dietary restrictions vs. favorite season ||
 | Vegetarian | Not vegetarian | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Fall | $6$ | $78$ | $84$
 | $8.4$ | $75.6$ | 
Spring | $9$ | $55$ | $64$
 | $6.4$ | $57.6$ | 
Summer | $15$ | $133$ | $148$
 | $14.8$ | $133.2$ | 
Winter | $6$ | $58$ | $64$
 | $6.4$ | $57.6$ | 
**Total** | $36$ | $324$ | $360$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of independence. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Dietary restrictions vs. favorite season ||
 | Vegetarian | Not vegetarian | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Fall | $6$ | $78$ | $84$
 | $8.4$ | $75.6$ | 
Spring | $9$ | $55$ | $64$
 | $6.4$ | $57.6$ | 
Summer | $15$ | $133$ | $148$
 | $14.8$ | $133.2$ | 
Winter | $6$ | $58$ | $64$
 | $6.4$ | $57.6$ | 
**Total** | $36$ | $324$ | $360$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(6-8.4)^2}{8.4}+...+\dfrac{(58-57.6)^2}{57.6} \\\\
&\approx 0.686+...+0.003 \\\\
&\approx 1.966
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=1.966$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{4}$ row variables and $\maroonD{2}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{4}-1)(\maroonD{2}-1)=3$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases sharply then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 3. A test statistic is marked before the right tail at chi squared = 1.97. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7b8541dbbf5e27814a4fff08def5fe36d7aff9f4)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=3$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=1.966$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The column for tail p = 0.25 and the row for d f = 3 are highlighted. 0.25 and 3 intersect at value 4.108.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6cddce176e926226ee05bfb13c3b0cd0892a208d)

Since our test statistic is between $0$ and $4.108$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is greater than $0.25$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>1.966$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.966 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 3 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.579
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=1.966; \\
&\text{P-value} > 0.25
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of researchers wondered whether there was an association between handedness and which foot was longest. They took a random sample of people, measured their feet, and recorded their handedness.  Here are the outcomes and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Handedness vs. longer foot ||
 | Ambidextrous | Left-handed | Right-handed | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Left foot | $6$ | $20$ | $94$ | $120$
 | $7.2$ | $13.2$ | $99.6$ | 
Right foot | $10$ | $13$ | $97$ | $120$
 | $7.2$ | $13.2$ | $99.6$ | 
Same length | $20$ | $33$ | $307$ | $360$
 | $21.6$ | $39.6$ | $298.8$ | 
**Total** | $36$ | $66$ | $498$ | $600$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of association. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Handedness vs. longer foot ||
 | Ambidextrous | Left-handed | Right-handed | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Left foot | $6$ | $20$ | $94$ | $120$
 | $7.2$ | $13.2$ | $99.6$ | 
Right foot | $10$ | $13$ | $97$ | $120$
 | $7.2$ | $13.2$ | $99.6$ | 
Same length | $20$ | $33$ | $307$ | $360$
 | $21.6$ | $39.6$ | $298.8$ | 
**Total** | $36$ | $66$ | $498$ | $600$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(6-7.2)^2}{7.2}+...+\dfrac{(307-298.8)^2}{298.8} \\\\
&\approx 0.2+...+0.225 \\\\
&\approx 6.622
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=6.622$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{3}$ row variables and $\maroonD{3}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{3}-1)(\maroonD{3}-1)=4$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases sharply then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 4. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 6.62. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b0b284ae2d3ed921a75d4b59d0ac9215fabeabf1)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=4$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=6.622$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.20 and 0.15 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 4 is highlighted. 0.20 and 4 intersect at value 5.989. 0.15 and 4 intersect at value 6.745.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8893b075b30c843374a4d142185727dca9d19c19)

Since our test statistic is between $5.989$ and $6.745$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.15$ and $0.20$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>6.622$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 6.622 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 4 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.157
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=6.622; \\
&0.15 <\text{P-value} < 0.20
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The owners of a large shopping complex wondered how their customers traveled to the complex. They surveyed a random sample of $100$ customers.  Here are the outcomes and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Transportation vs. purchase ||
 | Drive | Public transit | Other | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Made a purchase | $30$ | $25$ | $15$ | $70$
 | $35$ | $21$ | $14$ | 
No purchase | $20$ | $5$ | $5$ | $30$
 | $15$ | $9$ | $6$ | 
**Total** | $50$ | $30$ | $20$ | $100$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of independence. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Transportation vs. purchase ||
 | Drive | Public transit | Other | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Made a purchase | $30$ | $25$ | $15$ | $70$
 | $35$ | $21$ | $14$ | 
No purchase | $20$ | $5$ | $5$ | $30$
 | $15$ | $9$ | $6$ | 
**Total** | $50$ | $30$ | $20$ | $100$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(30-35)^2}{35}+...+\dfrac{(5-6)^2}{6} \\\\
&\approx 0.714+...+0.167 \\\\
&\approx 5.159
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=5.159$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{2}$ row variables and $\maroonD{3}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{2}-1)(\maroonD{3}-1)=2$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases vertically then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 2. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 5.16. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2eb9dbd270fcd170efb938c8535090aa788ea926)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=2$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=5.159$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.10 and 0.05 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 2 is highlighted. 0.10 and 2 intersect at value 4.605. 0.05 and 2 intersect at value 5.991.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ce7a4091219b3f95ff8093884ffd7f7dc741ce4d)

Since our test statistic is between $4.605$ and $5.991$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.05$ and $0.10$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>5.159$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 5.159 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 2 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.076
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=5.159; \\
&0.05 <\text{P-value} < 0.10
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The principal of a large middle school wondered whether students in each grade kept their lockers equally neat. They selected random samples of $30$ lockers belonging to students in each grade.  Here are the outcomes and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Grade vs. neatness ||
 | Neat | Messy | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
$6^{\text{th}}$ grade | $25$ | $5$ | $30$
 | $20$ | $10$ | 
$7^{\text{th}}$ grade | $17$ | $13$ | $30$
 | $20$ | $10$ | 
$8^{\text{th}}$ grade | $18$ | $12$ | $30$
 | $20$ | $10$ | 
**Total** | $60$ | $30$ | $90$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test for homogeneity. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Grade vs. neatness ||
 | Neat | Messy | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
$6^{\text{th}}$ grade | $25$ | $5$ | $30$
 | $20$ | $10$ | 
$7^{\text{th}}$ grade | $17$ | $13$ | $30$
 | $20$ | $10$ | 
$8^{\text{th}}$ grade | $18$ | $12$ | $30$
 | $20$ | $10$ | 
**Total** | $60$ | $30$ | $90$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(25-20)^2}{20}+...+\dfrac{(12-10)^2}{10} \\\\
&\approx 1.25+...+0.4 \\\\
&\approx 5.7
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=5.7$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{3}$ row variables and $\maroonD{2}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{3}-1)(\maroonD{2}-1)=2$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases vertically then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 2. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 5.70. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2ae68acad25c86fc367f0237c16a42c63eb1b12c)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=2$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=5.7$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The columns for tail p = 0.10 and 0.05 are highlighted, and the row for d f = 2 is highlighted. 0.10 and 2 intersect at value 4.605. 0.05 and 2 intersect at value 5.991.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ce7a4091219b3f95ff8093884ffd7f7dc741ce4d)

Since our test statistic is between $4.605$ and $5.991$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is between $0.05$ and $0.10$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>5.7$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 5.7 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 2 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.058
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=5.7; \\
&0.05 <\text{P-value} < 0.10
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of nutritionists wondered whether there was an association between gender and the kinds of food a person likes to eat. They surveyed a random sample of $231$ people about their favorite foods.  Here are the responses and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Favorite type of food vs. gender ||
 | Female | Male | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Meat | $37$ | $68$ | $105$
 | $55$ | $50$ | 
Produce | $16$ | $5$ | $21$
 | $11$ | $10$ | 
Starches | $54$ | $30$ | $84$
 | $44$ | $40$ | 
Other | $14$ | $7$ | $21$
 | $11$ | $10$ | 
**Total** | $121$ | $110$ | $231$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of association. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=23.635$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{4}$ row variables and $\maroonD{2}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{4}-1)(\maroonD{2}-1)=3$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases sharply then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 3. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at chi squared = 23.64. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3accb0e8347a99b589533ecfb9bb9a52f00cb0f8)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=3$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=23.635$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The column for tail p = 0.0005 and the row for d f = 3 are highlighted. 0.0005 and 3 intersect at value 17.730.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/48f8b947612efe9588977c4e5de0f6a960773ca6)

Since our test statistic is greater than $17.73$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is less than $0.0005$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>23.635$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 23.635 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 3 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.00003
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=23.635; \\
&\text{P-value} < 0.0005
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
An educational psychologist wondered whether there was a relationship between the amount of academic pressure a high school student felt and their plans for college. They surveyed a random sample of $300$ high school students throughout the country about their college plans and whether they felt academic pressure.  Here are the responses and partial results of a chi-square test (expected counts appear below observed counts):

|| Chi-square test: Planned years of college vs. feeling academic pressure now ||
 | $0$ years | Up to $4$ years | More than $4$ years | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Yes | $31$ | $48$ | $161$ | $240$
 | $31.2$ | $48$ | $160.8$ | 
No | $8$ | $12$ | $40$ | $60$
 | $7.8$ | $12$ | $40.2$ | 
**Total** | $39$ | $60$ | $201$ | $300$

They want to use these results to carry out a $\chi^2$ test of independence. Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**What are the values of the test statistic and P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

The chi-square statistic is an overall measure of how much the observed counts differ from the counts we expect if the null hypothesis is true. The P-value is the area above our test statistic in a chi-square distribution. The P-value represents a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of getting as much difference, or more, as we saw between the observed counts and the expected counts by chance alone?

###Chi-square test statistic formula

$\chi^2=\sum\dfrac{(\text{observed}-\text{expected})^2}{\text{expected}}$

###P-value

Use a chi-squared table or technology to estimate the area above the test statistic in a $\chi^2$ distribution with $\text{df}=\left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{rows}}-1 \right) \left( \overset{\# \text{ of}}{\text{columns}}-1 \right)$.

###$\chi^2$ test statistic

The table gave us the expected counts below each of the observed counts:

|| Chi-square test: Planned years of college vs. feeling academic pressure now ||
 | $0$ years | Up to $4$ years | More than $4$ years | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Yes | $31$ | $48$ | $161$ | $240$
 | $31.2$ | $48$ | $160.8$ | 
No | $8$ | $12$ | $40$ | $60$
 | $7.8$ | $12$ | $40.2$ | 
**Total** | $39$ | $60$ | $201$ | $300$

$\begin{align}
\chi^2&=\dfrac{(31-31.2)^2}{31.2}+...+\dfrac{(40-40.2)^2}{40.2} \\\\
&\approx 0.001+...+0.001 \\\\
&\approx 0.007
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###P-value

Our P-value is the area above our test statistic $\chi^2=0.007$ in a chi-squared distribution. Since there are $\purpleD{2}$ row variables and $\maroonD{3}$ column variables, we use $\text{df}=(\purpleD{2}-1)(\maroonD{3}-1)=2$.

![A right skewed chi square distribution curve starts at 0 on a horizontal axis representing chi squared. The curve increases vertically then decreases gradually across the axis. d f = 2. A test statistic is marked just to the right of the start at chi squared = 0.01. The area under the curve to the right of it is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f5438fcc99b85cba55776d12b42e2ad7105a5672)

We can use a table or technology to estimate the P-value.

**Using a table**

We can use a $\chi^2$ table to find the approximate area above our test statistic. We look across the row for $\text{df}=2$ for values near our test statistic $\chi^2=0.007$. We won't see our exact test statistic, and that's okay.

![A partial chi square table is displayed. The column for tail p = 0.25 and the row for d f = 2 are highlighted. 0.25 and 2 intersect at value 2.773.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2d0575102ed1eb7ebeab6ec8177c39c2941e1efb)

Since our test statistic is less than $2.773$, the corresponding tail area—our P-value—is greater than $0.25$.

**Using technology**

We can use the $\chi^2\text{ CDF}$ function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\chi^2>0.007$:

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2\text{ CDF} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.007 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\text{df: } 2 \\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.997
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\begin{align}
&\chi^2=0.007; \\
&\text{P-value} > 0.25
\end{align}$
